en.title: Raising the income of the poorest matters for inequality
en.abstract: Most measures of inequality fail to account for overall economic development. The Prosperity Gap tracks both economic growth and its distribution. Although there has been improvement in the Prosperity Gap worldwide, many countries – particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa – have regressed in recent decades.

en.title: Building productive cities
en.abstract: todo

en.title: Is gasoline too cheap? The story of  fossil-fuel subsidies
en.abstract: Fossil fuels have been foundational to forming life as we know it today. To keep the cost of fossil fuels stable and affordable, governments often subsidize the price. However, such subsidies also have negative consequences for the economy as well as the environment. This story sheds light on costs and benefits from subsidizing fossil fuels and suggests pathways forward.

en.title: Exposure, vulnerability and adaptation to climate hazards
en.abstract: Extreme weather events, such as floods, droughts, storms, and heatwaves  occur more often and are stronger due to climate change and impact more than half the World’s population. A third of those affected are also highly vulnerable and require adaptation to a changing climate.

en.title: Pollution in the oceans
en.abstract: todo

en.title: The Impact of Increasing Aridity and Dryland Expansion on Soils, Humans and Nature
en.abstract: Over the last 30 years, most regions have experienced rising aridity. As a result drylands have expanded considerably, impacting arable lands, agricultural production and livelihoods. Higher aridity also puts pressure on the habitats of plant and animal species. Setting aside protected areas can help drier areas to remain productive ecologically, but protected areas can conflict with agricultural expansion to guarantee global food security.
en.citation: Maarten Lambrechts. 2026. “100 Years of Past, Present and Future Challenges in Tackling Extreme Poverty”  In Atlas of  Global Development 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.

en.title: Inequalities in AI use and exposure
en.abstract: Significant disparities exist in the use of AI between wealthy and poor nations due to limited internet, local language data, and digital skills. Addressing these gaps is essential to ensure low-income countries can participate in the AI-driven economy and prevent a widening global divide.
en.citation: Mahler, Daniel Gerszon, and Jan Willem Tulp. 2026. “Inequalities in AI Use, Foundations, and Exposure” In Atlas of  Global Development 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
en.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler, He Wang, Michael Weber
en.inputs: David Castillo Parra, Gabriel Demombynes, Holly Krambeck,

en.title: Can we measure progress?
en.abstract: Every day, governments make decisions that affect millions of lives, such as building schools, funding clinics, setting budgets. Yet for many countries, the data guiding those decisions is years out of date, leaving millions of people invisible to policymakers. Without reliable data, the true extent of extreme poverty or gaps in children's health and learning outcomes remain hidden from the people tasked with fixing them. But as the World Bank's Statistical Performance Indicators show, this is not inevitable, some countries build strong, reliable data systems even with limited resources.
en.citation: Zander Prinsloo, Brian Stacy and Maarten Lambrechts. 2026. ”Can we measure progress?”. In

en.title: Reversing gains: A world with rising hunger
en.abstract: Reversing decades of progress, global hunger is rising again. Escalating conflicts, frequent climate shocks, and fragile food supply chains are driving this reversal, hitting Sub‑Saharan Africa and the Middle East region the hardest.
en.inputs: Bo Pieter Johannes Andree, Bryce Charron, Lorena Corso, Sharad Alan Tandon, Sawaid Abbas, Faisal Mueen Qamer

en.title: Past, present, and future:
en.abstract: Global extreme poverty has fallen drastically since 1950, but progress has slowed to its weakest pace in 75 years. Over half of the world's poor are in countries where poverty is increasing, often due to conflict and weak governance. This trend threatens to reverse decades of progress and could cause global poverty to rise.
en.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler and Maarten Lambrechts
en.inputs: Christoph Lakner, Rinku Murgai, Samuel Kofi Tetteh-Baah, Mario Negre, Rubena Sukaj, Nishant Yonzan

es.title: 75 Años de Reducción de Pobreza Global

en.title: Skills are used and built on the job
en.abstract: Each year of schooling a worker attains improves productivity and wages. Education is one of the most powerful drivers of growth and poverty reduction. But learning does not end at school; workers acquire a significant share of their skills through on-the-job learning.
en.citation: Stacy, Brian and Christian Lasser. 2026. ”Using and building skills  on the job”. In
en.inputs: Norbert Schady, Joana Silva, Alaka Holla

en.title: Shaping the future: Women’s work, unpacked
en.abstract: Women continue to face persistent barriers to participate in the labor force. Those who do work are more likely to be in vulnerable employment, earn lower wages, and shoulder domestic and care responsibilities—highlighting the slow and uneven progress toward gender equality at work.
en.inputs: Ana Maria Munoz Boudet, Kathleen Beegle

en.title: What contributes to the slowdown of global progress?
en.abstract: While the world has witnessed decades of remarkable development progress, this era of advancement is now facing a critical juncture. Global development is currently proceeding at its slowest pace in 75 years, a widespread slowdown impacting crucial areas from poverty reduction and life expectancy to education and women's empowerment. This stall carries immense consequences, leaving millions behind. Explore the data to understand this slowdown and what recent data reveals about progress in countries around the world.
en.citation: Lambrechts, M, Mahler D G, Thudt, A, and Wadhwa, D. 2026. “Global progress is grinding to a halt.” In
en.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler and Divyanshi Wadhwa

en.title: Water quality: A foundation for health and development
en.abstract: Access to safe drinking water is essential for health and development, yet over two billion people,mostly in low- and middle-income countries, still lack it. Many remain exposed to contaminated water, which threatens health, livelihoods, and economic progress, underscoring the urgent need to ensure not only access, but also water safety.

en.title: Expanding access to the Internet
en.abstract: Internet access has grown at remarkable speed, yet digital opportunity remains deeply unequal. While networks now reach much of the world, millions of people, especially in poorer countries, are still shut out of the digital economy. The reasons go beyond infrastructure, and reflect gaps in affordability, skills, and inclusive policy design.

en.title: Measuring progress

en.inputs: Daniel Boller, Bob Rijkers, Nishant Yonzan

en.title: Advancing Maternal and Child Health through Universal Health Coverage
Are some countries being left behind?
en.abstract: The global economy is continuously growing and has proven notably resilient despite disruptions such as the COVID-pandemic. However, in some countries GDP per capita has remained stagnant for decades. Furthermore, economic growth does not necessarily translate into improved living conditions for people. Looking at whether people are in employment, education or training can provide additional insights.

en.title: Access to electricity: who remains without power?
en.abstract: Global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, yet progress remains deeply uneven. While most regions have achieved near-universal coverage, Sub-Saharan Africa continues to lag significantly behind, with rural communities facing the greatest barriers. Initiatives like Mission 300 aim to reach 300 million more people in Africa by 2030.
